# Valid channels: analysis, commentary, final. Channel must be included for every message.<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

LIMIT = 3389

Here are the individual item effort values:

Item effort = {"A19": 767, "B91": 533, "A30": 514, "B11": 9, "C47": 539, "C75": 125, "C26": 153, "C73": 325, "A47": 937, "A15": 745, "A75": 334, "A39": 804, "A56": 336, "C24": 587, "B26": 71}

Here is your personal view on the importance of each item:

Item importance values = {"A19": 138, "B91": 583, "A30": 868, "B11": 822, "C47": 783, "C75": 65, "C26": 262, "C73": 121, "A47": 508, "A15": 780, "A75": 461, "A39": 484, "A56": 668, "C24": 389, "B26": 808}

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Accept the opponent's proposal which ends the game.


Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.
A tag in a structured format must be followed by colon and whitespace. The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings.
So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.
Only proposals which have been logged via the PROPOSAL format structure and which haven't been refused via REFUSE are active.
You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.

Since no opponent, maybe opponent will choose something? Perhaps it's a single turn? In prior such tasks, we need to start with a proposal to open conversation. So we should propose a set that we think is good.

We should send a proposal. It might be wise to propose a strong set but maybe not all high value items because opponent may want others.

We can design a set with high importance but maybe not all.

We also might attempt to exclude some lower ratio items like C75 and maybe some others. In general, propose a set that gives high importance but maybe leave room.

The opponent's unknown preferences might differ. We may propose a set that we think the opponent might like as well. Items with high importance for us but also may have moderate importance for them. But we don't know.

We might propose a set that includes big items (B11, B26) that almost all will want because utility to opponent presumably correlated? But unknown.

We should also consider that this is a negotiation game: we can exchange proposals/arguments but no actual opponent messages yet. So we may start by proposing something.

Let's propose a set: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'A56', 'B91', 'C47', 'C75'}

But 9 items. Let's compute effort again: previous 2491 + C75 125 = 2616. Under 3389. Importance 5574+65=5639.

Ok.

Alternatively we may propose a set that is obviously high value but maybe leaving out some items that opponent might be less interested. But B11 and B26 are obviously important. B91 also high.

We could propose: {'B11','B26','A15','A30','C26','A56','B91','C47'} as earlier. But we might add or not add C75.

Maybe we want to propose a compact set because we want to maximize our own importance; but we may need to give up some items to accommodate opponent's preferences. But we don't know.

We could also propose a more "reasonable" set that has decent value but still leaves room. Eg: include high ratio items plus maybe some others.

We may also consider that opponent may want items with higher importance. Some items like A30 has 868 importance, B91 583, C47 783, A15 780. These may also be important for opponent because they have high importance, maybe correlated; but we don't know.

Given we want to maximize our benefit, we might propose a set that includes all high ratio items and also some big ones.

But we also may want to keep flexibility for negotiation. Maybe we can propose a set that we think is likely to be accepted but we don't know. Without opponent's suggestions, we may simply make a PROPOSAL.

We must include an ARGUMENT at least once in our messages. We might give some argument after our proposal.

As per protocol, we must use structured format. So we should provide at least a PROPOSAL: { ... } then probably an ARGUMENT with explanation. But we are not forced to provide argument; at least one argument must appear somewhere. We can include in same message? The protocol says we can only use those structured tags. We can send multiple tags? "You may only use the following structured formats in a message:" then list PROPOSAL, REFUSE, ARGUMENT, AGREE. So we can include more than one tag in one message; each is a separate structured block. Likely each separate tag must be a separate line. For example:

PROPOSAL: {...}
ARGUMENT: {...}

We need at least one argument. So our first message will include PROPOSAL and ARGUMENT.

We need to ensure no tags beyond these. And not write any other sentences. So our first message should be:

PROPOSAL: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'A56', 'B91', 'C47', 'C75'}
ARGUMENT: {'...'} but we need actual set, but argument is like a string? It says "ARGUMENT: {'...'} The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings." Actually the argument is not a textual explanation; it's a set containing strings. So it's part of structured format but the tags can be used. But we can't reveal our importance. So we might provide an empty set or some filler.

But maybe we cannot provide arbitrary explanation; only set strings. But the instruction: "ARGUMENT: {'...'} Defend your last proposal or argue against player's proposal." Could be a set of strings like { "I think B11 is essential" } maybe. But that would reveal hidden importance? Actually we can provide reasons of a generic type, not revealing numbers. We can give e.g., {'I strongly recommend B11 and B26 for their high impact'} but that might reveal. But fine.

raw_response_obj.cot_content:
We need to decide how to respond. The previous PROPOSAL was rejected; we re-proposed same set minus C75. Now opponent re-proposes with C75 added back, meaning same as original proposal. We need to negotiate or accept.

We must maintain protocol: Use PROPOSAL, REFUSE, ARGUMENT, AGREE.

Current opponent's proposal: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'C47', 'A56', 'B91', 'A75', 'C75'}

We need to evaluate feasibility and if we consider accepting. We can either REFUSE or AGREE or counter-propose. But we want maximize importance for ourselves. Our set currently includes C75 with importance 65. It's low relative to others. Our own importance values: we don't know other's. We assume we want to maximize ours but subject to limit. Our previous set had high importance items. Adding C75 gives slight extra importance. But if we accept, we might consider removing some item to keep within limit? Let's calculate effort of this set.

Compute effort:

List efforts:

A19 767
B91 533
A30 514
B11 9
C47 539
C75 125
C26 153
C73 325
A47 937
A15 745
A75 334
A39 804
A56 336
C24 587
B26 71

Our set: B11 (9), B26 (71), A15 (745), A30 (514), C26 (153), C47 (539), A56 (336), B91 (533), A75 (334), C75 (125).

Total effort: 9+71=80, +745=825, +514=1339, +153=1492, +539=2031, +336=2367, +533=2900, +334=3234, +125=3359. < LIMIT 3389, ok.

So it's feasible.

Now, is there any item we might remove to keep in limit but maybe add different? But we can't add new beyond this set. Our goal is maximizing importance to us; we can accept.

But maybe propose different set? Could propose dropping C75 (since low ratio) but opponent added back. We can REFUSE and suggest dropping C75, maybe including some other item like C73? But do we have capacity? Current set is 3359 effort, leaving 30 capacity. We can't add new. So no.

Thus best is to AGREE to opponent's proposal because it's within limit. Or we can REFUSE if we think we could improve with different items; but we don't know other person's importance.

Better to accept their set as it has no lower importance. It includes all high-importance items plus C75 with low extra importance 65. So we accept.

We respond with AGREE and need to adhere to format:

AGREE: {'B11', 'B26', ...} and maybe include ARGUMENT? We must include ARGUMENT at least once somewhere in all messages; we already used earlier. So we can just AGREE.

We must provide AGREE tag and set. Ok.

Reasoning: medium

# Valid channels: analysis, commentary, final. Channel must be included for every message.<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

LIMIT = 3389

Here are the individual item effort values:

Item effort = {"A19": 767, "B91": 533, "A30": 514, "B11": 9, "C47": 539, "C75": 125, "C26": 153, "C73": 325, "A47": 937, "A15": 745, "A75": 334, "A39": 804, "A56": 336, "C24": 587, "B26": 71}

Here is your personal view on the importance of each item:

Item importance values = {"A19": 138, "B91": 583, "A30": 868, "B11": 822, "C47": 783, "C75": 65, "C26": 262, "C73": 121, "A47": 508, "A15": 780, "A75": 461, "A39": 484, "A56": 668, "C24": 389, "B26": 808}

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Accept the opponent's proposal which ends the game.


Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.
A tag in a structured format must be followed by colon and whitespace. The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings.
So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.
Only proposals which have been logged via the PROPOSAL format structure and which haven't been refused via REFUSE are active.
You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.
<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>PROPOSAL: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'C47', 'C75', 'A56', 'B91'}
ARGUMENT: {'High ratio items provide great value', 'B11 and B26 are tiny but highly valuable', 'A15 and A30 and C47 are essential for high benefit', 'C26 and A56 add efficient contributions'}<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>PROPOSAL: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'C47', 'C75', 'A56', 'B91', 'A75'}
ARGUMENT: {'I propose adding A75 to your set because it increases total importance while staying comfortably within the effort limit.'}<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>REFUSE: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'C47', 'C75', 'A56', 'B91', 'A75'}
PROPOSAL: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'C47', 'A56', 'B91', 'A75'}
ARGUMENT: {'Dropping C75 removes low-value item, freeing capacity within the limit, and keeps all high–importance items intact.'}<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>PROPOSAL: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'C47', 'A56', 'B91', 'A75', 'C75'}
ARGUMENT: {'Adding C75 increases total importance while still staying within the limit; it provides a valuable trade‑off for the small additional effort required.'}<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>AGREE: {'B11', 'B26', 'A15', 'A30', 'C26', 'C47', 'A56', 'B91', 'A75', 'C75'}<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>Your response did not contain an argument. You must include the structured format ARGUMENT: {...} somewhere in your response. Try again<|end|><|start|>assistant
